steps.title: Welcome to Day 9
steps.body: Yesterday you built a template. Today you learn how to chain tools together. One AI to summarize a document, another to draft a reply, another to check the tone — that is a workflow.

steps.title: A workflow example
steps.prompt: Here is a long article about [topic]. First, summarize it in 5 bullet points. Then, based on that summary, draft an email to my team explaining the key takeaways. Finally, check the email tone — is it clear and professional?
steps.primaryButton: That is three steps in one

steps.title: A real workflow
steps.body: You get a long email from a client. Step 1: Ask AI to summarize the email. Step 2: Ask AI to draft a reply. Step 3: Ask AI to check the tone. Each step builds on the previous one. That is a workflow.
steps.primaryButton: I can see how this works

steps.title: Workflow rules
steps.body: Rule 1: Each step should output something the next step can use. Rule 2: Add the output of each step to the next prompt. Rule 3: Use a separate chat thread for each workflow.

steps.title: Check your understanding
steps.question: What is the main advantage of a workflow?
options: It keeps everything in one long prompt
It breaks a complex task into manageable steps
It reduces the number of tools you need
feedback: Actually, workflows use multiple focused prompts.
Right. Each step does one thing well, and the next step builds on it.
Speed varies, but the main benefit is accuracy.
You might use more tools in a workflow, not fewer.

steps.title: Spot the workflow steps
steps.body: Find the three distinct steps in this request: Take this meeting transcript, list the action items, then write a follow-up email assigning each item to the right person, and finally check the email for clarity.

steps.title: Design your workflow

fields.label: What multi-step task do you face regularly?
Step 1: What information comes in first?
Step 2: What do you need to do with it?
Step 3: What output do you need?

steps.template: I need a workflow for {{task}}. Step 1: I receive {{step1}}. Step 2: I need to {{step2}}. Step 3: I need {{step3}} as the final output. Break this into a step-by-step AI workflow.

steps.title: Save your workflow
steps.body: Paste the workflow AI designed for you. You will use it tomorrow.

steps.title: Two tools, one task
steps.question: Run your workflow through your main AI tool. Then run the same task through a different AI tool. Which one handled the chain of steps better?

steps.title: Pro tip: Copy-paste between AIs
steps.body: Sometimes one AI is better at step 1, another is better at step 3. Do not feel locked in. Take the output from one tool and paste it into another. Mixing tools is not cheating — it is strategy.

steps.title: True or false?
steps.question: Once you start using one AI tool for a workflow, you should never switch to another tool mid-way.
feedback: Actually, switching tools mid-workflow can give you better results for each step.
Right. Different tools have different strengths. Mix them freely.

questions.label: What is the first workflow you will set up this week?
How many AI tools will your workflow use?

steps.title: Good fit for a workflow?
steps.question: Summarize a client email, draft a response, and check the tone.
feedback: Yes — this is a textbook multi-step workflow.
Exactly. Each output feeds the next step.

steps.title: Good fit for a workflow?
steps.question: Decide if I should buy a new car.
feedback: AI can help research, but the decision is yours alone.
Right. Use AI for research, but keep the decision for yourself.

questions.question: What is a workflow?
options: Chained steps where each step builds on the previous one

questions.question: It is okay to use different AI tools for different steps in the same workflow.
Breaking a complex task into smaller AI steps is called building a ______.

steps.title: Day 9 complete — Workflow Builder
steps.body: You now know how to chain AI tools into real workflows. Tomorrow you will learn how to use AI to automate repetitive tasks.
